1.summary: Urgent — Very limited M365 use. Teaching entirely paper-based. No CPD. Structural barriers present.
subCriteria: Very limited M365 access or use. Significant barriers (confidence, time, access, language). May not own a device.
Digital not integrated into teaching at all. Teaching approach unchanged by technology.
Very low confidence. No CPD engagement. Significant emotional barrier or structural constraint.
Teaching entirely on paper. Learners do not access Teams. No accountability for digital access.
HoA leadership involved. Device/access audit. Identify structural barriers requiring senior intervention.

2.summary: Challenged — Struggles with M365 basics. Minimal digital in teaching. Low confidence, avoids CPD.
subCriteria: Struggles with M365 basics. File management chaotic. Password/security gaps. IT support frequently needed.
Uses digital tools minimally. Accessibility not considered. May avoid digital teaching due to confidence.
Low confidence. Avoids CPD. May be resistant to change. Needs significant scaffolding.
Resources primarily paper-based or unshared. No Teams presence. Learners unclear how to access materials.
Intensive 1:1 foundation support (Accessibility Checker, Immersive Reader demo). Monthly DL check-in.

3.summary: Developing — Uses core tools but avoids new ones. Digital not always purposeful. CPD variable.
subCriteria: Uses core tools (Teams, email, basic file management). Avoids trying new tools. Cloud storage gaps.
Digital tools used but not always purposefully. Accessibility awareness low. Limited AT knowledge.
Hesitant about new tools. CPD engagement variable. May feel overwhelmed by pace of change.
Teams exists but underutilised. Resources shared ad-hoc. No evidence of AT promotion.
Weekly Foundations training (Jisc Discovery Tool results used). Agree one accessible practice this week.

4.summary: On Track — Confident core M365. Integrates digital purposefully. Engages with CPD. Some gaps remain.
subCriteria: Confident with core M365. File management and cloud storage routine. Occasional gaps with new tools.
Integrates digital tools purposefully. Aware of accessibility but inconsistent application.
Engages with CPD. Responsive to feedback. Some specific tool gaps or classroom confidence issues.
Teams used well. Most documents accessible. Some learners still accessing resources via email.
Fortnightly check-in. Agree one tool to master next. Buddy with Level 5 peer.

5.summary: Confident — Self-directed. WCAG 2.2 AA automatic. Seeks CPD. Mentors peers.
subCriteria: Reliable M365 use (Teams, OneDrive, email, accessibility tools). Cloud storage native.
Accessibility by design in all resources. WCAG 2.2 AA automatic. Immersive Reader & Live Captions in use.
Staff self-directed. Seeks out CPD. Comfortable adapting practice based on feedback.
Teams Assignments with rubrics. Accessible documents pre-shared. AT offered proactively.
Observe & celebrate. Ask DL to mentor peers. Document as case study for college.

1.summary: Urgent — Digital seen as others' problem. No strategy, no DL, digital absent from QIP.
subCriteria: Digital seen as a learner/staff problem, not an area leadership responsibility. Lack of ownership.
No digital strategy. DL not identified or actively resistant. Digital seen as IT responsibility.
Digital absent from QIP. No planned CPD. No digital initiatives underway.
No monitoring. No evidence. Uncertain impact of any interventions.
VP Quality or Ben Manning to meet HoA. Strategic partnership agreement needed. DPC to facilitate monthly check-ins.

2.summary: Challenged — Needs acknowledged but not understood. No strategy, DL not identified or reluctant.
subCriteria: Digital needs acknowledged but not understood. Multiple competing priorities obscure digital focus.
No clear digital strategy. DL either not identified or reluctant. Budget not allocated.
Digital not in QIP. CPD links unclear. No specific digital events planned.
No systematic monitoring. Data not collected. Difficult to know if interventions working.
DPC leads gap analysis conversation. HoA & DPC jointly write one-page digital plan for next 3 months.

3.summary: Developing — Aware of digital gaps but not clearly articulated. DL identified, role unclear.
subCriteria: Aware of digital as an issue but gaps not clearly articulated. Relies on DPC to identify.
Wants digital improvement but plan not yet written. DL identified but role unclear.
Digital mentioned but not a priority target. CPD activities not yet matched to Pyramid.
Ad-hoc conversations. No systematic data collection. Unsure how to measure progress.
DPC supports HoA to write gap analysis. DL trained on Health Check framework. Weekly DPC check-in.

4.summary: On Track — Identifies key gaps. Committed. Some planning. DL identified. Digital in QIP.
subCriteria: Can identify key gaps (e.g., staff access to software, learner baseline skills). Partially data-informed.
Committed to digital improvement. Some planning in place. DL identified but role not fully resourced.
Digital priority named in QIP. Some CPD activities linked. Teach Meets planned.
Fortnightly check-ins with DPC. Some data monitoring. Intention to link to learner outcomes.
DL supported with Health Check framework. HoA reviews fortnightly. Progress fed back to DPC.

5.summary: Confident — Clearly articulates gaps. Owns strategy. DL role clear and resourced. Monthly reviews.
subCriteria: Clearly articulates digital gaps in area (staff, learners, infrastructure). Evidence-based from Jisc/Century data.
Owns digital strategy. Plans documented in QIP. Budget allocated. DL role clear and resourced.
Digital target in QIP. CPD activities linked to Pyramid level. Teach Meets scheduled.
Monthly progress review with DPC. Data dashboard used (Jisc/Century). Learner outcomes linked to digital skills.
DL leads Health Checks independently. HoA reviews data monthly. Reports progress to Quality cycle.

1.summary: Urgent — Critical device and connectivity failures. Teaching severely constrained.
subCriteria: Significant device shortages. Learners and staff regularly unable to access digital tools.
Unreliable or absent WiFi in teaching spaces. Connectivity disrupts lessons routinely.
Critical software absent or unlicensed. Key curriculum tools inaccessible.
AT tools absent. No Read&Write, Immersive Reader, or accessible software available.
IT support unavailable or extremely slow. Issues unresolved for weeks. No escalation pathway.

2.summary: Challenged — Significant gaps in devices or connectivity. Key software missing.
subCriteria: Device availability inconsistent. Shared devices cause delays and access inequity.
WiFi issues in some teaching spaces. Occasional lesson disruption due to connectivity.
Some specialist or curriculum software unlicensed. Workarounds required.
Some AT tools available but not consistently deployed or known.
IT support slow. Issues may take a week or more to resolve. Workarounds required.

3.summary: Developing — Adequate baseline but gaps exist. WiFi inconsistent in some areas.
subCriteria: Adequate devices for most activities but specific gaps exist (e.g., no laptops in workshop).
WiFi generally functional but issues in specific rooms or corridors.
Core software available. Some specialist tools missing or on older versions.
AT tools available but staff awareness of what's available is low.
IT support available. Response times variable. Some recurring issues unresolved.

4.summary: On Track — Good provision. Reliable access. Most software available. Minor gaps being addressed.
subCriteria: Good device provision for staff and learners. Rare access issues.
Reliable WiFi in most teaching spaces. Rare connectivity disruption.
Most required software licensed and up to date. Minor gaps identified.
AT tools available and staff know how to access them. Some proactive use.
IT support responsive. Most issues resolved within 48 hours. Good working relationship.

5.summary: Confident — Excellent provision. Reliable everywhere. All software available. Proactive IT partnership.
subCriteria: Full device provision. Staff and all learners have reliable access at all times.
Fast, reliable WiFi across all teaching spaces. Rarely an issue.
All required software licensed. Specialist and industry-standard tools accessible.
AT fully integrated. Read&Write, Immersive Reader, Live Captions all routinely available.
IT support proactive. Relationship with IT team is collaborative. Issues anticipated and resolved quickly.

1.summary: Urgent — Century not set up. No baseline data. Digital skills development invisible.
subCriteria: Century not set up for area. No baseline digital skills data collected.
No awareness of specialist digital skills needed. No tracking system in place.
No progress monitoring of learner digital skills whatsoever.
Digital skills development completely invisible in learner tracking.
VP Quality conversation needed. Set up Century immediately. HoA & DPC co-create specialist skills plan.

2.summary: Challenged — Under 50% Century IA. No specialist skills mapped. No progress monitoring.
subCriteria: Less than 50% learners completed Century IA. Dashboard not accessed. Limited understanding of data.
Specialist skills not formally mapped. No assessment criteria or tracking system.
No systematic progress monitoring of learner digital skills.
Learners unaware of their digital skills level.
HoA priority: Century completion plan (what is blocking?). Map top 3 skills. DPC training.

3.summary: Developing — 50–70% Century IA. Specialist skills informal. Limited monitoring.
subCriteria: 50–70% learners completed Century IA. Limited dashboard access or understanding.
Specialist skills identified informally. No formal assessment criteria. Tracking ad-hoc.
Limited progress monitoring. Unsure how to measure specialist skills progression.
Learners may not know their starting point. Progress not transparent.
Drive Century completion to 80%+ this week. Draft one specialist skills rubric. Set up Markbook tracking.

4.summary: On Track — 80%+ Century IA. Key specialist skills identified. Quarterly review.
subCriteria: 80%+ learners completed Century IA. Dashboard accessed quarterly. Some follow-up diagnostics run.
Key specialist skills identified. Some assessment criteria drafted. Tracking method agreed.
Quarterly Century review. Some specialist skills progress tracked informally. Plan to formalise.
Learners know their starting point. Progress monitoring beginning. Some AT identified at baseline.
Ensure remaining learners complete Century by Week 2. Embed rubric in Markbook. Monthly check-ins.

5.summary: Confident — 100% Century IA. All specialist skills mapped. Monthly reviews. Learner progress transparent.
subCriteria: 100% learners completed Century IA. Dashboard monitored termly. Gaps identified; interventions in place.
All specialist skills formally mapped per qualification. Clear rubrics tracked in Markbook.
Monthly progress review from Century. Specialist skills progression evidenced (portfolios, assessments).
Learners can see starting point & current level. Progress transparent. AT needs identified early.
Share dashboard data with peers. Co-design assessment rubrics. Lead specialist skills mapping training.

1.summary: Urgent — Digital not integrated. Paper-based only or digital just for submission.
subCriteria: Digital not integrated into curriculum planning. Assignments paper-based or digital only for submission.
Digital use minimal. Technology not connected to learning design.
Accessibility not considered. Barriers for SEND/dyslexia/language learners not addressed.
AT not offered or discussed. No mention in assignment briefs.
Strategic review needed. Why is digital not part of curriculum design? Unit redesign workshop required.

2.summary: Challenged — Digital used as add-on. Sporadic or compliance-driven. No accessibility thought.
subCriteria: Digital skills not explicitly part of assignment design. Used as add-on to paper tasks.
Digital tools used sporadically or as compliance ('post to Teams because we're digital').
Limited thought given to accessibility. One submission format. Barriers not identified.
AT not part of assignment planning. No mention in briefs.
HoA & DPC co-lead unit redesign. Make one assignment fully digital-first with accessible design.

3.summary: Developing — Some digital; purpose unclear. Single format. AT rarely mentioned.
subCriteria: Some assignments are digital; others paper-based with no clear rationale for the choice.
Digital tools used but purpose sometimes unclear. 'Tech as engagement' rather than essential.
Limited consideration of multiple means of engagement. Submission often one format only.
AT rarely mentioned in assignments. Not offered proactively.
Co-design one unit. Make digital essential to one assignment. Add accessible submission options. Weekly DPC.

4.summary: On Track — Most assignments digital with emerging outcomes. Generally accessible. AT known.
subCriteria: Most assignments include digital skills. Learning outcomes emerging; not yet explicit in all.
Digital is mostly purposeful but some 'nice-to-have' tools used without clear rationale.
Assignment designs generally accessible but not systematically checked for UDL.
AT mentioned in some assignments. Immersive Reader known; not always offered proactively.
Review one unit. Identify 2–3 assignments to redesign for explicit digital skills + UDL. Monthly coaching.

5.summary: Confident — All assignments have clear digital outcomes. UDL applied. AT embedded in instructions.
subCriteria: All assignments have clear digital learning outcomes explicitly mapped to assessment criteria.
Tool choice is purposeful. Could not deliver same learning without digital. No 'tech for tech's sake'.
Multiple means of representation, action, engagement (UDL). Submission formats flexible.
AT embedded in assignment instructions. Students offered choice of tools. Captioning provided.
Mentor peer teachers on UDL assignment design. Lead Teach Meet on accessible assignments.

1.summary: Urgent — Significant digital poverty. Learners struggling with basic digital literacy.
subCriteria: Many learners lack home devices. Cannot access digital resources outside college.
Learners struggling with basic digital literacy. Very low confidence with required tools.
No digital poverty mitigation in place. Barriers unacknowledged.
High teacher mediation required. Learners cannot independently navigate digital learning.
Device loan scheme or bursary referral urgent. Baseline assessment of access needed immediately.

2.summary: Challenged — Digital poverty affects a minority. Low confidence across cohort. Little mitigation.
subCriteria: A minority of learners lack home device access. Affects equity of learning outside college.
Learner confidence low across significant part of cohort. Specific tool barriers evident.
Some awareness of digital poverty but no formal mitigation or referral pathway.
Learners need frequent support to use required digital tools independently.
Identify learners with access issues. Begin device loan or bursary process. Weekly check-in.

3.summary: Developing — Some access/confidence barriers. Area aware. Mitigation partial.
subCriteria: Some learners face access barriers at home. Issue acknowledged but not systematically addressed.
Learner confidence variable across cohort. Some manage independently; others need significant support.
Partial mitigation in place (e.g., college devices available at set times). Not systematic.
Most learners can navigate required tools with occasional teacher support.
Map access issues. Review bursary uptake. Scaffold digital tasks more explicitly this term.

4.summary: On Track — Most learners have access and basic confidence. Digital poverty being addressed.
subCriteria: Most learners have reliable home device access. Rare access issues identified and addressed.
Learner confidence generally good. Most manage required tools independently.
Device loan or bursary process in place for learners with access issues.
Learners generally independent with required digital tools. Minimal teacher mediation.
Monitor remaining access issues. Embed digital skills scaffolding in lessons for confidence building.

5.summary: Confident — Learners digitally independent. Access issues minimal and mitigated. Strong engagement.
subCriteria: All or almost all learners have reliable home device access. Digital poverty actively mitigated.
High learner confidence with required digital tools. Self-directed digital learners evident.
Robust device loan/bursary pathway. No learner left without access due to poverty.
Learners fully independent with digital learning. Choose and adapt tools for their own needs.
Learners can articulate their digital skills journey. Peer support visible. Celebrate and share.

1.summary: Urgent — No accessibility awareness. AT completely absent. Learners with needs unsupported.
subCriteria: No awareness of accessibility standards. Resources shared without any consideration.
AT completely absent from practice. Learners with access needs unsupported.
No variation in delivery. Rigid format. Barriers for ESOL, SEND, dyslexia not addressed.
Learners with access needs are struggling or disengaging.
VP Quality escalation. SEND/AT specialist audit. Emergency Teach Meet needed. This is urgent for learner outcomes.

2.summary: Challenged — Checker rarely used. No captions. AT rarely mentioned or offered.
subCriteria: Accessibility Checker rarely used. Resources shared without checking. No captions on videos.
AT rarely mentioned. Not offered proactively. Learners unaware it exists.
Single format delivery. Limited choice or flexibility for learners.
Learners struggle to access digital resources. High reliance on teacher mediation.
Intensive support. HoA involvement. Liz Burkley/Lois Knight to co-facilitate accessibility training.

3.summary: Developing — Awareness exists but not systematic. AT offered to SEND only. Single delivery format.
subCriteria: Accessibility awareness exists but not yet systematic. Some resources pass checker; others don't.
AT offered to learners with formal SEND provision only. Not proactively offered to all.
Limited variation in representation or action. Most lessons follow single delivery format.
Some learners struggle to access resources independently. Reliance on teacher support.
Run Accessibility Checker demo + practice (30 mins). Fix one resource type this week. Weekly DPC.

4.summary: On Track — Most resources accessible. AT offered to some. Some UDL variation. Becoming proactive.
subCriteria: Most resources accessible. Accessibility Checker known but not yet automatic. Some lack alt text/captions.
AT offered to some learners; becoming more proactive but not yet universal offer.
Some variation in how content is presented. Learners have some choices but not consistent.
Most learners can access resources. Some still struggle with independence or resource discovery.
Agree on accessibility standard this week. Run one team training on Checker. Monthly DPC check-in.

5.summary: Confident — All resources accessible. AT proactively offered to all. UDL routine. Learners independent.
subCriteria: All shared resources pass Office Accessibility Checker. Alt text on images. Contrast checked. Captions standard.
AT proactively offered to all learners (not just SEND). Immersive Reader, Live Captions, Checker mentioned routinely.
Clear multiple means of representation, action, and engagement (UDL). Options visible in every lesson.
Learners confidently navigate accessible resources. Independence visible. AT use is normalised.
Model accessibility practice for other areas. Document process. Lead accessibility Teach Meet.

1.summary: Urgent — No DL identified. No Health Checks. No evidence. No peer support.
subCriteria: No DL identified. Area leadership unclear on who should play this role.
No Health Checks. No observation framework. No eyes-on-ground evidence.
No peer support. DL role not understood by anyone in the area.
No evidence collected. Nothing logged.
Immediate escalation to HoA + VP Quality. Jointly identify DL THIS WEEK. Emergency DL induction.

2.summary: Challenged — DL identified but reluctant/overwhelmed. No Health Checks. No evidence.
subCriteria: DL identified but reluctant or overwhelmed. Role unclear; support lacking.
No Health Checks happening. Observation framework not understood.
Not engaging in peer support. May be withdrawn from the role.
No systematic evidence collection. Nothing documented.
DPC intensive 1:1 support. Simplify DL role (start with one observation per month). Pair with confident DL mentor.

3.summary: Developing — DL identified but role unclear/unresourced. Health Checks not started or sporadic.
subCriteria: DL identified but role unclear or not yet resourced. May feel uncertain about expectations.
Health Checks not yet started or sporadic. Observation framework not yet clear.
Not yet coaching peers. May be focused on own learning.
Inconsistent reporting. Evidence not yet collected systematically.
Weekly DPC meeting to clarify DL role. Co-design first Health Check. Training on framework. Start weekly reports.

4.summary: On Track — DL identified and understood. Health Checks beginning. Supporting 1–2 peers.
subCriteria: DL identified and generally understood. Role beginning to be resourced.
Health Checks beginning; fortnightly+ schedule. Observations structured. Proformas completed.
Supporting 1–2 peer staff. Visible in area. Some peer-led activity.
Fortnightly reports to DPC. Most Health Checks documented. Evidence filing consistent.
Monthly DPC check-in. Agree on Health Check schedule. Mentor one peer DL. Sustain momentum.

5.summary: Confident — DL role protected and valued. Monthly+ Health Checks. Mentoring peers. Weekly reports.
subCriteria: DL clearly identified, understood, and valued by area. Role is protected time and resourced.
Health Checks running independently. Monthly+ schedule. Observations systematic & evidence-based.
Mentoring 2+ peer DLs or newer staff. Visible as leader in area. Peer-led training happening.
Weekly reports to DPC. Health Check proformas completed. Evidence logged in DPC Impact Hub.
Lead Health Check training for other areas. Help DPC design framework. Document role for case study.
